description: Master file operations in C++ using the fstream library for persistent data storage.

slides.title: Stream to File 📂
slides.content:
# The `<fstream>` Library

C++ simplifies file handling using Streams, just like `cin` and `cout`.

## Three Main Classes
1.  **`ofstream`:** Output File Stream (Write data).
2.  **`ifstream`:** Input File Stream (Read data).
3.  **`fstream`:** File Stream (Read and Write).

slides.title: Writing to a File
Reading from a File
Class Check
slides.content: Which class is specifically for READING files?

slides.title: Why is_open()?
File Modes
slides.content:
# Opening Modes 🎛️

You can specify how to open a file via the second argument.

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| `ios::in` | Open for reading (Default for ifstream) |
| `ios::out` | Open for writing (Default for ofstream) |
| `ios::app` | Append to end of file |
| `ios::binary` | Open in binary mode |

```cpp
// Append mode
ofstream file("log.txt", ios::app);
file << "New Entry\n";
```

slides.title: Full Example: Note Saver
Mode Check
slides.content: Which mode prevents overwriting existing file content?

slides.title: Summary
slides.content:
# Recap: File Handling in C++ 🎓

Persistence made object-oriented.

## Key Takeaways
- **`<fstream>`:** The header you need.
- **`ofstream`:** Uses `<<` to write.
- **`ifstream`:** Uses `>>` or `getline` to read.
- **`is_open()`:** Always check this.
- **Modes:** `ios::app` helps in logging without deleting old data.

Files are great, but for complex data, professional apps often use Databases!
